question: [File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that System.nanoTime() is used for the deadline (not wall clock), and on some JVM implementations nanoTime() can wrap around or behave unexpectedly after long uptime, causing the deadline check while (System.nanoTime() < deadline) to behave incorrectly and either loop forever or exit immediately, causing total network shutdown?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the application startup is fully blocked in waitForDatabase() during the retry loop, by triggering a database outage precisely when the mirror node is restarting (e.g., during a rolling update), causing the startup to block for the full 5-minute timeout and creating a prolonged window where no transactions are confirmed, causing total network shutdown?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the IllegalStateException thrown by waitForDatabase() propagates up through the Spring DataSource bean initialization (CommonConfiguration.dataSource()), causing the entire Spring application context to fail to start, which prevents all downstream beans including transaction processors from initializing, causing total network shutdown?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the ImporterConfiguration.flywayDataSource() also calls dbWaiter.waitForDatabase() with the owner credentials, by causing the database to accept the owner connection but reject the application user connection, so the Flyway datasource succeeds but the main datasource fails, leaving the application in a partially initialized state that causes total network shutdown?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that DriverManager.getConnection() is called with the full jdbcUrl including any embedded credentials, and the caught exception's getMessage() is logged via log.info(), potentially leaking the database password in plaintext to log aggregation systems accessible to the attacker, enabling credential theft and subsequent database manipulation causing total network shutdown?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the retry loop has no circuit breaker or jitter in Uninterruptibles.sleepUninterruptibly(properties.getInterval()), so multiple mirror node instances restarting simultaneously all retry at the same fixed interval, creating a thundering herd that overwhelms the database with synchronized connection attempts and causes total network shutdown?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the jdbcUrl passed to DriverManager.getConnection() is not validated for scheme (e.g., jdbc:h2:, jdbc:hsqldb:, jdbc:sqlite:), so if an attacker can influence the Spring datasource URL configuration, they can redirect the connection to an in-memory or file-based database that always succeeds, causing the application to start against a wrong database and fail to process transactions, causing total network shutdown?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the Properties object jdbcProperties is populated with user, password, connectTimeout, and socketTimeout, and then passed to DriverManager.getConnection(), where a JDBC driver that supports property injection via URL parameters could allow the attacker to override these properties by embedding conflicting parameters in the jdbcUrl, bypassing timeout protections and causing total network shutdown?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the connectTimeout is set to 2 seconds by default and socketTimeout to 2 seconds, but these are passed as JDBC driver properties that may be ignored by certain proxy or pgpool configurations, causing the actual connection attempt to block for the OS-level TCP timeout (typically 2 minutes), making each retry take 2 minutes and exhausting the 5-minute deadline in only 2-3 retries, causing total network shutdown?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the exception catch block catches all Exception types (not just SQLException), so if DriverManager.getConnection() throws an OutOfMemoryError or other Error subclass that is not caught, it propagates immediately out of waitForDatabase() without the retry logic, crashing the application startup and causing total network shutdown?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the retry loop catches Exception but not Throwable, so if the JDBC driver throws an Error (e.g., NoClassDefFoundError for a missing driver class), it bypasses the retry logic and propagates directly, causing the application to crash on first startup attempt and causing total network shutdown?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the connection opened by DriverManager.getConnection() in the try-with-resources block is a real database connection that consumes a server-side connection slot, and if the database has a low max_connections setting, the repeated retry connections from waitForDatabase() can exhaust all available slots, preventing legitimate application connections and causing total network shutdown?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the isEnabled() check at the start of waitForDatabase() returns immediately if disabled, and if an attacker can influence the hiero.mirror.common.databaseStartup.enabled configuration to false (e.g., via environment variable injection in a container environment), the application starts without waiting for the database, immediately fails on first DB operation, and causes total network shutdown?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the interval between retries is fixed at properties.getInterval() (default 2 seconds) with no randomization, by timing a database availability window to be shorter than 2 seconds, so the database is available between retries but unavailable during each retry attempt, causing the loop to always fail and throw IllegalStateException, causing total network shutdown?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the jdbcUrl is passed directly to DriverManager.getConnection() without checking for SSRF-enabling schemes, by injecting a URL like jdbc:postgresql://internal-metadata-service:5432/db to redirect the connection probe to an internal cloud metadata endpoint, leaking cloud credentials that enable further attacks causing total network shutdown?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the IllegalStateException message includes 'Database did not become available within ' + properties.getTimeout(), and this exception is logged and potentially surfaced in health check endpoints, to confirm that the database is unreachable and time a coordinated attack that keeps the database unavailable beyond the timeout, causing total network shutdown?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the retry loop in waitForDatabase() holds the Spring context initialization thread blocked, preventing liveness probes from responding, causing Kubernetes to kill and restart the pod, which triggers another waitForDatabase() call, creating an infinite restart loop that permanently prevents the application from starting and causing total network shutdown?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/util/DatabaseWaiter.java -> Scope: Critical: Network not being able to confirm new transactions (total network shutdown)'] [Function: waitForDatabase()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that DriverManager.getConnection() is a synchronized method in some JVM implementations, so if multiple threads call waitForDatabase() concurrently (e.g., due to multiple DataSource beans), they serialize on the DriverManager lock, causing thread starvation and blocking all application initialization threads, causing total network shutdown?
